sourceMovie.title: Beetlejuice
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit Beetlejuice for the same reason it broke through in 1988: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a comedy rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Tim Burton keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The fantasy and comedy blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Ghostbusters
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Ghostbusters shares Beetlejuice's gift for fantasy — the 1984 film delivers comedy without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: The Addams Family
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes Beetlejuice compelling — the fantasy, the comedy — shows up in The Addams Family (1991) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that funny storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: Monster House
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The fantasy energy of Beetlejuice is alive in Monster House (2006), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a funny payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: Beetlejuice Beetlejuice
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Beetlejuice Beetlejuice (2024) operates in the same fantasy as Beetlejuice, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The dark payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

recommendations.title: Nimona
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Nimona (2023) belongs to the same conversation as Beetlejuice — both anchor their fantasy in comedy emotion that doesn't let go. The funny undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: Haunted Mansion
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Beetlejuice's fantasy stayed with you, Haunted Mansion (2023) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The comedy through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: Corpse Bride
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Beetlejuice excels in fantasy, Corpse Bride (2005) doubles down on romance to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: Doctor Sleep
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Doctor Sleep (2019) is the natural next step after Beetlejuice: same fantasy DNA, different perspective. The dark streak and horror elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: My Girl
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Beetlejuice who respond to its comedy will find My Girl (1991) immediately familiar. Both films share a drama sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: A Haunted House 2
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Beetlejuice fans keep landing on A Haunted House 2 (2014) — the comedy is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

faq.question: What should I watch after Beetlejuice?
faq.answer: Try Ghostbusters, The Addams Family, Monster House—each shares DNA with Beetlejuice while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Beetlejuice?
faq.answer: Beetlejuice (1988) is best described as Fantasy, Comedy. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Beetlejuice part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Beetlejuice Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
